shower.thought: We spend our whole lives learning how to be ourselves, but we never get to see who we actually became until we're gone.
Your childhood feels infinitely long because you were experiencing everything for the first time—compression only happens in memory.
The people you dislike most are usually just showing you what you're afraid of becoming.
You'll never know if you made the right choice because you'll never get to live out the other option.
Forgetting something is just your brain deciding it wasn't important enough to keep, which is both efficient and heartbreaking.
Most of your suffering comes from comparing your behind-the-scenes to everyone else's highlight reel, yet you do it anyway.
The things you're most certain about are probably the things you've thought about the least.
You're the most honest version of yourself in the moments right after you wake up, before the day tells you who to be.
Every conversation you have is teaching someone something about who you are, whether you mean it to or not.
The only real difference between a hobby and a waste of time is whether you enjoy it—but you still judge yourself for both.
Your phone knows you better than you know yourself, which is comforting and horrifying simultaneously.
We call it "getting our lives together" as if our lives were ever apart in any way that matters.
Time moves slower when you're paying attention and faster when you're not, so living well might literally be living longer.
The people who say they don't care what others think are usually just better at hiding how much they do.
You're probably the antagonist in someone else's story, and they're the protagonist in theirs, and somehow that's not devastating—it's just honest.
Regret is just nostalgia for the version of you that made a different choice.
The most dangerous people aren't the ones who think they're right—they're the ones who think they've already figured out why they're wrong.
You can't actually know if the way you experience color or taste or love is the same as anyone else, yet you talk about these things like everyone's living in the same reality.
Most of your problems would be solved by either better sleep, more money, or someone who cares—and you know exactly which one you're refusing to fix.
The moments you remember most vividly are often the ones that seemed ordinary when they were happening.

motivational.thought: The obstacle in your path is not blocking your way — it is the way. — Ancient Proverb
Small progress is still progress. Celebrate it. — Unknown
You cannot control the wind, but you can adjust your sails. — Dolly Parton
The people who succeed are not the ones without fear. They're the ones who do it anyway. — Unknown
Your mistakes are proof you tried. Don't apologize for that. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing what you want most over what you want now. — Unknown
The only way out is through. — Robert Frost
You are not waiting to become ready. You become ready by beginning. — Unknown
Mediocrity is a choice. So is excellence. Choose again. — Unknown
Regret weighs more than effort. Remember that when you hesitate. — Unknown
What lies behind us and what lies before us are tiny matters compared to what lies within us. — Ralph Waldo Emerson
The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
Your value is not determined by productivity. But your growth is determined by effort. — Unknown
Everything you want is on the other side of fear. — Jack Canfield
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is now. — Ancient Proverb
You don't need to see the whole staircase, just the next step. — Martin Luther King Jr.
Suffering is temporary, but regret is forever. — Unknown
Success is not the absence of failure. It's the persistence after it. — Unknown
The person you become is worth more than the comfort you leave behind. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you right now. Make them proud. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my dentist my teeth were getting worse.
He said that's what happens when you stop flossing and start stress eating during a pandemic.

My friend said he's been going to the gym every day for a year.
I said "That's dedication."
He said "No, that's my court-ordered community service."

I asked my accountant if I could write off my depression as a business expense.
He said only if it's generating revenue.
I said it's generating a lot of crying, does that count?

My mom asked why I never call her anymore.
I said I do, but she's usually asleep.
She said she's awake now.
I said I know, I'm calling from the landline.

I went to a therapist who specialized in childhood trauma.
Turns out I didn't have childhood trauma—I just had a childhood.

My boss told me I needed to be more of a team player.
I asked if that meant sharing my Netflix password or my antidepressants.
He said the password would be fine.

I told my friend I was thinking about getting a tattoo.
He said "Don't do it, you'll regret it."
I said "I already regret most of my life decisions, what's one more?"

My doctor asked if I exercise regularly.
I said "Does running away from my problems count?"
He said "Only if you're actually getting somewhere."
I said "I'm not."

I asked my ex why she left me.
She said I wasn't supportive of her dreams.
I said I would've been if any of them seemed realistic.

My therapist asked what my biggest fear is.
I said dying alone.
She said "Most people fear that."
I said "Yeah, but I'm actively working toward it."

I went to a self-help seminar titled "Find Your Purpose."
Turns out my purpose was to find the exit.

My friend became a life coach.
I asked him what qualified him for the job.
He said his own life fell apart spectacularly, so now he helps others avoid his mistakes.
I said "Are you succeeding?"
He said "Not really, but I charge by the hour."

I told my parents I was considering a career change.
My mom said "But you've only had this job for three months."
I said "Exactly, I'm getting out while I still have a chance."

My coworker said she's never had a bad day in her life.
I asked if she was on something.
She said "Denial. It's wonderful."
I said "Can I get some?"
She said "You're already on it, you just don't realize it."

I went to a dating app where it asks "What are you looking for?"
I put "Someone who won't notice I'm slowly becoming my father."
I got zero matches.

My barber asked how I wanted my hair cut.
I said "Like I'm about to make some life-changing decisions but I'm too tired to actually make them."
He said "Say no more."

I asked my lawyer what my chances were in court.
He said "Let's just say I'm updating my LinkedIn."

My friend said he's on a spiritual journey.
I asked where it was taking him.
He said nowhere, but the airport wifi is good.

I told my HR department I needed mental health days.
They said I could take as many as I needed.
I said "Great, I'll be back never."
They said "That's the spirit, don't forget to use the out-of-office auto-reply."

My neighbor asked why I never go outside anymore.
I said the odds of running into someone who knows me were too high.
He said "But I know you."
I said "Yeah, and this is awkward, isn't it?"
